name: Premature Scaling with Hidden Churn
narrative: Founders mistake gross growth for net growth. MRR looks healthy because new customers mask churn. When acquisition slows — and it always does — the hidden churn becomes a cliff. By the time the problem is visible in top-line metrics, runway is gone.

warning_signals.signal: NPS declining while MRR grows
Support ticket volume increasing month-over-month
Power user cohort engagement declining
CAC trending upward for 2+ months
Expansion revenue flat despite growing customer base

survival_playbook: Pause all hiring immediately — freeze burn while you diagnose
Run a 30-day customer development sprint with recently churned users
Identify your top 3 churn drivers — fix these before growing again
Get NPS above 45 before re-engaging any acquisition channels
Reduce burn multiple below 1.5x before next scale attempt
Implement a formal health score system for all accounts

famous_failures.detail: Grew from $10M to $100M ARR then collapsed. Hidden churn masked by hypergrowth for 18 months.
10x growth, then rapid collapse when unit economics never worked at scale.
Expanded to 7 cities while losing money on every single order.

name: Runway Optimism Bias
narrative: Founders consistently underestimate how long fundraising takes and overestimate their next milestone's fundraisability. 8 months of runway feels like plenty until month 6 when you're in conversations and month 8 when you're out of cash.

warning_signals.signal: Runway below 9 months with no term sheet
MRR growth rate declining for 2+ consecutive months
Burn increasing while growth slows
Key hires departing

survival_playbook: Start fundraising process immediately — not when runway hits 6 months
Extend runway: cut burn to absolute minimum to get to 12+ months
Identify and double down on your single highest-leverage growth channel
Reach default alive: get to cash-flow neutral within 6 months
Talk to 50+ investors before you need money, not after

famous_failures.detail: Extreme case — ran out of credibility before cash.
$25M raised, burned through it without shipping, couldn't raise again.

sources: Paul Graham 'Default Alive or Default Dead'

name: Product-Market Fit Mirage
narrative: Early customers said yes out of politeness, friendship, or curiosity — not genuine need. Retention is weak, expansion is nonexistent, and referrals don't happen. The founders convince themselves this is a onboarding problem, a pricing problem, or a feature problem. It's a fit problem.

warning_signals.signal: Churn above 10% monthly from month 3+
No organic referrals or word-of-mouth
NPS below 20
Users not completing core workflow
LTV:CAC ratio below 2x

survival_playbook: Stop selling — talk to your 5 most active users and understand exactly why they stay
Define your 'must-have' user: the person who would be 'very disappointed' if product disappeared
Rebuild product specifically for that user — cut everything else
Re-launch only to that specific segment with explicit targeting
Do not scale until monthly churn is below 3% for 3 consecutive months

famous_failures.detail: $41M raised, launched a social photo app nobody wanted. Shut down in 2012.
Superior product to Spotify in many ways — but lost on distribution and network effects.

sources: First Round 'Knowing When You Have PMF'

name: CAC Exceeds LTV at Scale
narrative: The business looks great in a spreadsheet and terrible in reality. Early customers were cheap to acquire (warm intros, personal networks). As you scale into paid channels, CAC increases and you discover the unit economics never actually worked.

warning_signals.signal: CAC increasing 2+ months in a row
LTV:CAC ratio falling below 3x
Payback period extending beyond 18 months
Marketing spend growing faster than revenue

survival_playbook: Stop paid acquisition entirely — find the 1-2 organic channels that work
Rigorously calculate true fully-loaded CAC including all sales and marketing costs
Increase LTV: improve retention, add upsells, increase prices for new customers
Find adjacent customers you can reach more cheaply
Do not resume paid acquisition until LTV:CAC is consistently above 4x

famous_failures.detail: Daily deal economics fell apart as CAC rose and retention collapsed.
Flash sale model works once — repeat customers don't come back at same CAC.

name: Co-Founder Conflict at Inflection
narrative: Co-founder conflicts are the #1 reason YC companies fail according to PG. The conflict usually surfaces at inflection points: first major hire, first enterprise customer, first pivot decision. Each co-founder's vision for the company diverges as stakes get real.

warning_signals.signal: Co-founders avoiding direct communication
Parallel decision-making without alignment
Equity or role disputes surfacing
Team members reporting to different co-founders on same issues

survival_playbook: Have the hard conversation now — before it becomes unfixable
Engage a neutral third party: a board member, advisor, or executive coach
Establish explicit decision rights: who has final say on what
If irresolvable: negotiate a buyout before the conflict destroys value
Bring in a COO to manage operational conflicts between technical/commercial visions

famous_failures.detail: Parker Conrad forced out, company culture collapsed.
Reggie Brown lawsuit over co-founder status cost years and millions.

sources: Paul Graham 'The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups'

name: Pivot Without Signal
narrative: Founders pivot out of desperation, not data. They abandon a product that might have worked with 6 more months of iteration for something new that feels exciting. The new direction has even less validation than the original, and runway is now shorter.

warning_signals.signal: Strategic direction changing monthly
Team morale declining due to constant change
Investor confidence declining
No clear hypothesis being tested — just activity

survival_playbook: Before pivoting, define exactly what metric would prove your current product works
Give the current approach 60 more days with a specific, testable hypothesis
If pivoting, pivot toward your existing strongest customers — not away from them
A pivot to adjacent use case has 3x better success rate than a full pivot
Never pivot twice in less than 6 months — it signals lost conviction

famous_failures.detail: Multiple pivots, lost focus, eventually sold for scraps.
Anonymous social app pivoted multiple times, shut down returning $6M to investors.

name: Bridge Round Death Spiral
narrative: A bridge round buys time but not survival. If the underlying metrics don't improve, the next round is even harder to raise. Each bridge creates overhang, complicates the cap table, and signals distress to later investors. Most companies that take 2+ bridge rounds don't survive.

warning_signals.signal: First bridge round raised without hitting milestones
Growth rate not improving post-bridge
Lead investors not participating in bridge
Team departures accelerating post-bridge

survival_playbook: Take bridge only if you have a clear, specific milestone that will enable the next raise
Cut burn to minimum sustainable levels before taking bridge
Have explicit agreement with bridge investors on what success looks like
Consider acqui-hire as alternative to second bridge
If lead investors won't bridge, that's a signal — take it seriously

famous_failures.detail: Raised $900M+ across many rounds and bridges, still failed.
Launched, failed to gain traction, shut down in 6 months.

name: Premature International Expansion
narrative: International expansion before domestic PMF. Each new market requires localisation, compliance, support, sales — a completely new go-to-market. Companies that expand internationally before their core market is healthy almost always stretch themselves too thin.

warning_signals.signal: Domestic NPS still below 50
International revenue below 5% of total after 6 months
Operational complexity increasing faster than revenue
Team spending >30% of time on international without results

survival_playbook: Pause international expansion entirely until domestic NPS exceeds 55
Pick ONE international market and go deep — not shallow in 5
Hire a local country manager who owns P&L before expanding
Set a 12-month deadline: if international doesn't reach X% of revenue, exit
Use international revenue targets — not headcount — as expansion trigger

famous_failures.detail: $7B investment, shut down in 2 years. Core US model not ready for export.
Burned $2B before selling to DiDi. Wrong market timing.

name: Platform Dependency Collapse
narrative: Building on top of a platform (Facebook, Salesforce, Shopify, Twitter) that can kill your business with a policy change or by building the feature themselves. Every major platform has done this — it's not 'if' but 'when'.

warning_signals.signal: >70% of revenue dependent on single platform
Platform launching competing native features
Platform changing API terms or pricing
Platform acquiring a competitor

survival_playbook: Immediately begin reducing platform dependency to below 50% of revenue
Build a direct channel: email list, native app, direct sales
Differentiate along dimension the platform cannot easily copy
Engage with platform BD team — become a strategic partner not a parasite
Pursue channel diversification as a top-3 company priority

famous_failures.detail: 98% Facebook dependent. Facebook changed algo, Zynga lost 90% of value.
Twitter shut it down. Entire business on one platform.

name: Feature Creep Stagnation
narrative: The product tries to be everything to everyone and ends up being nothing to anyone. Each enterprise customer gets custom features. The roadmap is driven by whoever shouts loudest. Core product becomes unusable for the original ICP while new prospects don't see the value either.

warning_signals.signal: More than 30% of features used by less than 5% of users
Enterprise custom requests consuming >40% of engineering
Onboarding time increasing quarter over quarter
Sales cycle lengthening due to product complexity

survival_playbook: Freeze all new feature development for 90 days
Audit: remove or hide every feature used by less than 5% of active users
Redefine your ICP with radical specificity — who exactly is this for?
Rebuild onboarding from scratch for the new ICP
Create a 'no-new-features' policy for custom enterprise requests

famous_failures.outcome: Survived by going back to basics
famous_failures.detail: Famous for removing features and improving product.
Feature bloat made product unusable while simpler competitors won.

name: Hiring Ahead of Revenue
narrative: Team grows 3x while revenue grows 1.3x. It feels good — momentum, ambition, the right 'grown up company' signaling. Then you need to raise again and realize the only thing that grew was your burn rate. Reducing headcount is expensive, morale-destroying, and public.

warning_signals.signal: Headcount growth rate 3x+ revenue growth rate
Burn multiple above 3x
Manager layers being added before revenue justifies them
New hires not ramped within 60 days

survival_playbook: Hiring freeze immediately until burn multiple falls below 2x
Audit every open role: must be tied to specific revenue or retention outcome
Consider: can you hit next milestone with current team plus process improvements?
Implement a 90-day ramp plan for every hire — no more 'figuring it out'
Rule: don't hire for a role until you're doing that job yourself and it's clearly limiting growth

famous_failures.detail: Headcount and spending wildly ahead of sustainable revenue.
IPO'd, built huge team, never had viable unit economics.

name: Well-Funded Competitor Entry
narrative: A large, well-funded player (incumbent or well-capitalized startup) enters your market. If you haven't built deep defensibility — proprietary data, network effects, unique distribution — you will lose a war of attrition. Most startups don't survive this.

warning_signals.signal: Salesforce/HubSpot/insert-giant launching similar feature
VC-backed competitor raising $20M+ Series A
Churn starting to mention competitor by name
Win rate declining in competitive deals

survival_playbook: Move upmarket or downmarket immediately — pick a lane the giant won't follow
Double down on your unique data or workflow integrations they can't replicate
Accelerate customer lock-in: deeper integrations, data gravity, switching costs
Find 3 enterprise customers for whom you are mission-critical — protect them at all cost
Position as the specialist vs. their generalist — own a use case completely

famous_failures.detail: Microsoft bought Yammer as Teams competition grew.
Google acquired to kill competition for Gmail.

sources: a16z 'Why Software is Eating the World'

name: Negative Net Revenue Retention
narrative: Net Revenue Retention below 100% means your existing customers are worth less each year. Even if you keep acquiring new customers, you're running on a treadmill. Businesses with NRR below 100% cannot build durable value — every customer cohort decays.

warning_signals.signal: Expansion revenue not covering churn revenue
Average contract value declining in renewal cohorts
Downgrades more common than upgrades
Customers not discovering new features

survival_playbook: Calculate NRR by cohort — find which segments are below 100% and why
Build a customer success function focused exclusively on expansion revenue
Introduce a usage-based pricing tier that grows with customer success
Identify your top 10% of customers by expansion — understand what makes them different
Kill features and segments where NRR is permanently below 90%

famous_failures.detail: Ratings/reviews SaaS with contracting enterprise contracts.
Enterprise social, high churn as Slack entered market.

name: Enterprise Sales Trapped in SMB
narrative: SMB customers love the product but their LTV is too low to build a sustainable business. The company tries to move upmarket to enterprise, but the product, process, and team aren't ready. Enterprise deals take 9-18 months, SMB churn continues, and the company runs out of runway in the transition.

warning_signals.signal: Average ACV below $5K with high churn
Enterprise pilots converting below 30%
Sales cycle exceeding 6 months for first enterprise deals
Product not ready for enterprise security/compliance requirements

survival_playbook: Pick a lane: commit to SMB with volume model OR enterprise with ACV >$25K
If enterprise: hire an enterprise AE with 3+ references before building pipeline
Raise prices for SMB to improve unit economics — lower volume, better economics
Build enterprise requirements (SSO, audit logs, SLAs) before starting enterprise sales motion
Give yourself 24 months for the enterprise transition — it's slower than you think

famous_failures.detail: SMB scheduling tool tried to move to enterprise, ran out of runway in transition.
Classic pattern documented in SaaS benchmarks.

name: Founder-Market Fit Loss
narrative: The founder built what they personally needed, and that was enough to get to $1-2M ARR. But the market has moved, the ICP has shifted, and the founder is no longer the target customer. Growth stalls because the founder's intuition — once their superpower — is now leading them in the wrong direction.

warning_signals.signal: Founder spending less time with customers
Product roadmap driven by intuition not data
Win rates declining in new customer segments
NPS dropping among newest customer cohorts

survival_playbook: Do 20 customer interviews this month — not with old customers, with newest churned ones
Hire a Head of Product who is closer to the current customer than you are
Separate your personal opinions from user research — document everything
Consider whether the company needs a different type of leader for this phase
Build a systematic customer feedback loop — stop relying on founder intuition alone

famous_failures.detail: Multiple leadership issues rooted in founder vision vs. market reality.
Founders built what they wanted, not what the mass market needed.

sources: Ben Horowitz 'The Hard Thing About Hard Things'
